explicit_text: I was born in the Mae Kai Key refugee camp in 1998, which is located between the Thai and Burmese borders. My parents came from Burma and settled there after fleeing their home country. I lived in Mae Kai Key until I was four years old, experiencing a community where people sang carols and engaged in many activities. Unfortunately, in 2002, a flash flood destroyed the camp, forcing my family and many others to relocate.
implicit_text: I was born in a refugee camp located between the Thai and Burmese borders in 1998. My parents came from Burma and settled there after fleeing their home country. I lived in that camp until I was four years old, experiencing a community where people sang carols and engaged in many activities. Unfortunately, in 2002, a flash flood destroyed the camp, forcing my family and many others to relocate.

explicit_text: Thailand was where the Mae Kai Key refugee camp was located, right near the border with Burma. My family lived in refugee camps in Thailand for many years after fleeing Burma. It was in Thailand that we faced the flash flood in 2002 that destroyed our camp and forced us to move to Mae La Oon camp. Thailand was the place where my early childhood was shaped by the refugee experience.
implicit_text: The refugee camp where we lived was located in a Southeast Asian country near the border with Burma. My family stayed in camps in this country for many years after fleeing Burma. It was there that we faced the flash flood in 2002 that destroyed our camp and forced us to move to another camp nearby. This country was the place where my early childhood was shaped by the refugee experience.

explicit_text: After the flash flood in 2002 destroyed Mae Kai Key, my family and I had to move to the Mae La Oon refugee camp. This camp became our new home where we stayed for several years. Life there was difficult, and as I grew older, I began to understand the hardships my parents faced and why we had to live in refugee camps. Mae La Oon was a place of survival and transition for us before we eventually moved to the United States.
implicit_text: After the flash flood in 2002 destroyed our first refugee camp, my family and I had to move to another camp nearby. This new camp became our home where we stayed for several years. Life there was difficult, and as I grew older, I began to understand the hardships my parents faced and why we had to live in refugee camps. That camp was a place of survival and transition for us before we eventually moved to the United States.

explicit_text: In 2013, my family decided to move to Saint Paul, Minnesota, after hearing that many Karen people were resettling there and living better lives. We arrived in November 2014 after a long process of interviews, tests, and learning about the United States. Saint Paul became our new home where I learned English and started to build a future with dreams and goals. Although we had relatives here, many friends and family remained behind in Burma and the camps.

explicit_text: My parents originally came from Burma, which is why we had to flee and live in refugee camps along the border in Thailand. Burma is the country we left behind due to difficult circumstances, and many of our relatives still live there. Some family members chose to stay in Burma because they were afraid of starting a new life elsewhere or had to care for elderly parents. Burma is the homeland that shaped my family's history and the reason for our refugee journey.

explicit_text: My family and I moved to the United States because we wanted a better life and more freedom. We heard that many Karen people had resettled here and were doing well, so in 2013 we began the long process of interviews and screenings. We arrived in November 2014 and settled in Saint Paul, Minnesota. The United States gave me a chance to learn English, pursue education, and dream about my future, even though leaving behind friends and family was very hard.

We arrived in November 2014 and settled in a city in Minnesota. explicit_text: In September 2002, a devastating flash flood hit the Mae Kai Key refugee camp where I lived. It was raining and thundering heavily, and I remember hearing people screaming and shouting for help. Many people lost their lives that day, and because of the flood, my family had to move to a new camp called Mae La Oon. That event changed my life and forced us to leave the only home I had known up to that point.
